focus: Determine whether canonical-first is the correct universal contract and identify concrete regressions for every legacy-first consumer or persisted-path dependency found in iteration 1.
noveltyJustification: All 5 findings are new regression analyses built from iteration 1's inventory, separating safe canonical-first precedence from unsafe canonical-only behavior.
keyQuestions: Is canonical-first the correct universal contract, and which legacy-first consumers or persisted paths could regress under that change?
answeredQuestions: Is canonical-first the correct universal contract, and which legacy-first consumers or persisted paths could regress under that change?
ruledOut: Canonical-only resolution because it would orphan unique legacy-only packets
Bulk persisted-ID rewriting because inspected identities are metadata-backed or root-relative
Shared-resolver reordering as a fix for direct specs/ writers such as untracked spec/create.sh

text: Canonical-first is the correct unqualified default only with explicit-root preservation and legacy fallback retained.
Distinct duplicate packet identities switch winner; symlink aliases, canonical-only layouts, and legacy-only layouts keep the reachable object.
Bare child/folder/session resolution changes only on duplicates; containment-only consumers do not retarget, and diagnostics reorder.

Unqualified generate-context writes switch duplicate or new fallback targets to canonical, while explicit and exact existing paths remain stable; downstream workflow follows the resolved absolute target.
Inspected packet and workflow identities are metadata-backed or root-relative, so root alias spelling does not require migration; divergent duplicate metadata follows the selected packet.
Preserve explicit rooted inputs and dual-root legacy fallback while making canonical win unqualified collisions.
Canonical-only resolution would make unique legacy-only packets unreachable to shared consumers.
No root-spelling persistence was found in the inspected packet-id or workflow folder-identity paths.
